meta.title: eazip.ch - Password-protected ZIPs in your browser
meta.description: Create password-protected ZIP files right in your browser. AES-256 encryption, free, no upload, no account. Your files never leave your device.

hero.kicker: Made in Switzerland · Runs in your browser
hero.subtitle: The easiest way to make a password-protected ZIP file. Drop your files, pick a security level, and download the archive, without sending anything to anyone.

form.unsupportedBody: Please open eazip.ch in a recent version of Chrome, Edge, Firefox or Safari to make ZIPs of any size.
form.statusAddFile: Add at least one file to get started.
form.statusSavedFs: Saved "{filename}" to the location you chose.
form.errorGeneric: Something went wrong while building the ZIP.
form.errorUnsupported: Your browser is too old to stream large ZIPs to disk. Please use a recent Chrome, Edge, Firefox or Safari.

dropzone.subnote: Multiple files welcome. Nothing leaves your device.

none.title: No password
none.blurb: A plain ZIP. Anyone with the file can open it.

legacy.title: Legacy password
legacy.blurb: Opens everywhere (macOS, Windows). Weak: fine for casual privacy, not secrets.

modern.title: Modern password
modern.blurb: AES-256 encryption. Real security. Recent macOS opens it natively; Windows recipients may need 7-Zip or a similar tool.

password.label: Password
password.generateHint: Generate creates a 5-word passphrase: easier to remember than random characters and still extremely strong.
password.warnLegacy: Legacy ZipCrypto is weak. Use this only for casual privacy, not for sensitive data.
password.warnModern: AES-256. Recent macOS handles this natively in Finder. On Windows, the recipient will need 7-Zip, WinRAR or another modern archiver; the built-in unzipper can't open it.

info.title: Everything happens in your browser.
info.intro: eazip.ch never uploads your files to a server. Your computer compresses and encrypts them locally, and the only thing that leaves the page is the finished ZIP, going directly into your Downloads folder.
info.noneBody: A regular ZIP. Anyone can open it without a key. Pick this when you just want to bundle files together, for example to email a few photos or share a folder.
info.legacyBody: The old ZipCrypto format. Opens on every computer, including macOS Finder and Windows Explorer, without installing anything. But it's cryptographically weak: a determined attacker can crack the password.
info.modernBody: AES-256 encryption (the WinZip AE-2 standard). Genuinely secure: a strong password is unbreakable in practice. Recent macOS opens it natively in Finder; Windows recipients will need a modern archiver.
info.guideNone: Just sharing files? Use No password. ZIPs shine at bundling multiple files into one; no need for encryption you don't want.
info.guideLegacy: Sending to a non-technical person and the data isn't sensitive? Use Legacy password. They'll be able to open it on any computer with the password you give them. Good enough for tax documents, personal photos, casual paperwork.
info.guideModern: Protecting something that actually matters? Use Modern password. Recent macOS opens AES-256 ZIPs natively in Finder. On Windows, your recipient will need 7-Zip or WinRAR. On Linux, any standard archiver works. On older macOS, point them at Keka.
info.passwordsPara1: Send the password through a different channel than the ZIP itself. If you email the ZIP, send the password by SMS, Signal, or in person. A password sitting in the same inbox as the file it protects is no protection at all.
info.passwordsPara2: Click Generate for a 5-word passphrase like Brave-Tiger-Lemon-Sunset-Wheat. It's far easier to read and retype than random characters, and it's still strong enough that no realistic attacker can guess it.
info.privacyItem1: Your files never leave your device.
info.privacyItem2: Compression and encryption run entirely in your browser.
info.privacyItem3: No analytics, no tracking, no account.
info.privacyItem4: Works offline once the page is loaded.
